subject: Astronomy & Space
title: Supernovae: Exploding Stars
description: A quick, practical guide to Supernovae: Exploding Stars.

hook.text: When a massive star dies, it explodes with the energy of an entire galaxy—briefly outshining billions of stars.

buildup.text: The core runs out of fuel and collapses in milliseconds. The outer layers slam inward, then rebound outward.

discovery.text: In 1054, Chinese astronomers recorded a guest star so bright it was visible in daylight for 23 days.

twist.text: Supernovae forge elements heavier than iron—gold, platinum, uranium. Every heavy atom on Earth was born this way.

climax.text: The remnant of the 1054 supernova is the Crab Nebula. At its center spins a neutron star 30 times per second.

punchline.text: Stars die to make gold. We are built from their debris.

quiz.question: Where do elements heavier than iron, like gold and uranium, come from?
options: They are forged in the extreme energy of supernova explosions
They form slowly inside the cores of red giant stars
They are created by cosmic rays bombarding interstellar dust
